title: Molecular-level study of the effect of nitrogen addition levels on soil dissolved organic matter composition in a desert steppe of northwest China.
author_string: Wang, Yan, Xue, Bin, Fu, Longwei, Du, Lingtong, Ma, Kun et al.
abstract: Dissolved organic matter (DOM) plays a key role in grassland carbon biogeochemistry and shows sensitivity to global climate change, particularly nitrogen (N) deposition. We investigated the soil DOM molecular composition by UV-Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy, and FT-ICR MS through a N addition experiment (CK, N5, N10, N20, and N40 [0, 5, 10, 20, and 40 g N m-2 year-1, respectively]) in a desert steppe of northwest China. Moderate N inputs (N5-N20) caused a dose-dependent increase in DOM content (9.39-19.05 % increase). Under low N input (N5-N10), microbial consumption reduced the content of labile peptide-like compounds in DOM while stimulated fulvic acid production. High N input (N20-N40) suppressed fulvic acid formation but resulted in the accumulation of peptide-like compounds through a reduction in microbial metabolism. The readily degradable molecules (e.g., lipids and aliphatics) decreased due to the preferential utilization of N-rich substrates. This coincided with the accumulation of recalcitrant compounds (lignins/carboxyl-rich alicyclic molecules (CRAMs)-like and aromatics), reflecting a shift in microbial carbon use efficiency toward stable byproducts under N-induced oxidative stress. Notably, the peak accumulation of lignins/CRAMs-like at N10 (12 % increase) suggests an optimal N level for the microbial production of stabilized metabolites before inhibitory effects dominate at higher N loads. Additionally, available nitrogen (AN) and available potassium (AK) emerged as the key factors influencing the complexity of soil DOM. Appropriate N input (N10 and N20) promoted soil DOM accumulation by enhancing the proportion of recalcitrant components in the desert steppe soil, which reduced microbial mineralization rates and increased net DOM retention.
keywords: Fourier transform ion cyclotron resonance mass spectrometry
